titleStmt.title: The Secretary of State to the Ambassador in France ( Caffery )Repeated to Moscow as telegram 15; to London as telegram 61.

div.head: The Secretary of State to the Ambassador in France ( Caffery )Repeated to Moscow as telegram 15; to London as telegram 61.
div.p:
28. Prior to November 24, United States side Combined Coal Committee, Washington, following up earlier suggestions, raised again the desirability of going forward with the establishment of an organization to deal with continental coal problems during the transitional period. December second, British Embassy, Washington, proposed
Foreign Relations, 1944, vol. ii, p. 631. European Coal Organization composed in the first instance of U.S., U.K., U.S.S.R. and European Allies with provision for a linkup with Allied Control Commissions, Combined Boards, UNRRA,United Nations Relief and Rehabilitation Administration. EITO,European Inland Transport Organization; for documentation, see pp. 1389 ff.
UMA,United Maritime Authority. representatives of employees and employers in industry and consuming interests, including the interests of neutrals. An interim ECOEuropean Coal Organization. was proposed, subject of course to military necessities. November 25, Ronald,Nigel Bruce Ronald, British Acting Assistant Under-Secretary of State. in London, asked HawkinsHarry C. Hawkins, Counselor for Economic Affairs at the American Embassy, London. what we thought about the time when the French should be asked to join the talks.See telegram 10405, November 25, 1944, from London, Foreign Relations, 1944, vol. ii, p. 631. December 7, Department replied tentatively that it was not at that time appropriate to include the French and exclude other European Allies.See telegram 10251, December 7, 1944, 10 p.m., to London,
ibid., p. 635. December 20, British Embassy told Department British expected to deliver copy of coal memorandum of December second to French in Paris and would be glad to know our reaction, On further consideration we agree that French should be approached now.

Embassy London has proposed, however, that whole procedure should be changed for both EECEuropean Economic Committee. and ECO and that the U.S., U.K., U.S.S.R. and French should be asked to a meeting in late January to consider what questions in economic affairs of Europe, and coal in particular, in transition period are not adequately cared for by present machinery, and what new machinery is desirable. Agenda suggestions could be discussed informally in advance and agreed on at meeting. Each country would be requested to send an observer if not prepared to participate fully. This would avoid obvious difficulties shown so clearly in EITO history, until the representatives of four nations had at least met.

We have therefore presented a memorandum along these lines to the British and Soviet Embassies here, and will present a similar one to the FrenchJanuary 12, 1945, not printed. as soon as you notify us of the delivery of the British coal memorandum to the French in Paris.Delivered to the Minister for Foreign Affairs on January 7, 1945. You are authorized to say to the French at that time that we agree heartily with their joining the EEC and the ECO, although we wish to discuss further some of the details of the British proposals, and that they will hear from us in the near future.
Text of memorandum to be delivered to French here follows in next succeeding telegram as number 29.Not printed.
